summary: Stancil argues that foundational LLM vendors face a brutal cost structure where model improvement is a perpetual, escalating expense, not a one-time investment. Because compute is leased and model quality decays quickly against fast-moving and open-source competitors, their moats are thin and short-lived, making survival depend on constant fundraising or massive revenues. The ultimate winners, he suggests, will be whoever is ahead when market hype and capital dry up—not necessarily the best or fastest builders.
money_quotes: Every LLM vendor is eighteen months from dead.
The billions that OpenAI spent on building prior versions of GPT is not, because better versions of it are already available for free on Github.
An LLM vendor that doesn’t spend tens of millions of dollars a year—and maybe billions, for the leaders—improving their models is a year or two from being out of business.
The market needs to be irrational for you to stay solvent.
And the winners won’t be who ran the fastest or reached some finish line, but whoever was leading when the market decided the race is over.
key_insight: LLM vendors lack durable moats because model advances are costly, quickly commoditized, and easily matched, so survival hinges on relentless funding or massive revenues rather than technological lead alone.
